items.q: What does the 'all-or-nothing' principle mean for action potentials?
items.correct: Once threshold is reached, the action potential fires at full size — sub-threshold stimuli produce no action potential.
items.misconception: Stronger stimuli produce larger action potentials.
items.random_wrong: Action potentials get bigger as they travel along the axon.
items.why: Students think 'more stimulus = bigger response' (analogous to volume control), missing that stimulus strength is coded by FREQUENCY of action potentials, not amplitude.

items.q: During an action potential, do Na+ and K+ move through the same channels?
items.correct: No — voltage-gated Na+ channels open first (depolarisation, Na+ in), then voltage-gated K+ channels open (repolarisation, K+ out).
items.misconception: Na+ and K+ swap places through the same channel at the same time.
items.random_wrong: Only Na+ moves during an action potential; K+ stays still.
items.why: Students simplify ion movement as 'they swap' rather than recognising two separate channel types opening sequentially.

items.q: What is the purpose of the refractory period after an action potential?
items.correct: It ensures action potentials travel in one direction only and limits the maximum firing frequency.
items.misconception: The refractory period exists because the cell runs out of energy and needs a rest.
items.random_wrong: The refractory period blocks all signals from reaching the neurone.
items.why: Students think of it as a 'rest break' rather than a functional feature ensuring unidirectional propagation.

items.q: Approximately what is the membrane potential at the peak of an action potential?
items.why: It's +30 mV. “-70 mV” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What causes the depolarisation phase of an action potential?
items.why: It's Na+ moves into the cell. “K+ moves into the cell” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If the resting potential is -70 mV and the peak is +30 mV, what is the total change in membrane potential?
items.why: The real figure is 100. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
